Task id: T78 (ledgerRefs goals:G5) — ROUND 2 re-dispatch after review.
Branch implement/T78 (HEAD 12a238a), worktree /home/pavel/work/safe/fecalia/.claude/worktrees/agent-a6ff3c7de32c86767. Work THERE if reachable; else `git switch -c t78-fix implement/T78` in your own worktree via the shared .git store, commit, report the EXACT sha (copy from git output).
Go repo — gate `go build ./... && go vet ./... && go vet -tags realhosts ./test/realhosts/... && go build -tags realhosts ./test/realhosts/... && go test ./...` via `nix develop -c sh -c '...'`.

ONE prior-round criticism (autonomously-fixable LOGIC ERROR):
The valid_name subtest's RESOLUTION report is derived SOLELY from the "hub failover: first endpoint resolution" journal marker (internal/device/failover.go:302), which fires ONLY on the R70 DEFERRED-install path (updateResolution boot adoption when every spec booted EMPTY). But on the standing testbed the valid name (o3.7mind.io) resolves during the Q30 bounded BOOT resolve (resolveBootEndpoints, default 5s dns timeout), which is SILENT on success (only failures log — device.go:884/890). So the happy-path subtest would DETERMINISTICALLY report 'RESOLUTION: "o3.7mind.io" did not resolve...' alongside 'HANDSHAKE: OK' — the central datum this tier exists to report is WRONG on the primary scenario.

FIX (cover BOTH resolution paths): source the actually-installed peer endpoint from DAEMON STATE rather than only the R70 journal marker. Options (pick the cleanest that fits the realhosts harness):
- Read the installed engine-peer endpoint from the daemon's UAPI (IpcGet `endpoint=` line) — the same mechanism T101's sessionMonitor / T74's deviceInstallEndpoint use; OR
- Read it from the metrics plane if exposed; OR
- Treat a COMPLETED HANDSHAKE on a name-dialed endpoint as resolution success, sourcing the endpoint from daemon state, AND still parse the R70 marker when present (deferred path).
Keep it REPORT-ONLY (t.Logf only on the outcome path; no t.Fatalf/Errorf on resolution/handshake/traffic — that contract must stay intact). The report must be CORRECT on BOTH the Q30-boot-resolve happy path AND the R70 deferred-install path. Do NOT fix the base lint redness (D45, out of scope).

Re-run the gate incl. -tags realhosts vet/build. Commit, return the structured result json {taskId, status, resultCommit, branch, filesTouched, checkSummary, summary, blockedReason?} with the exact resultCommit sha.

- baboon: Baboon data model compiler reference. Covers model syntax, multi-version evolution, code generation, wire-format conversions, database migrations, backward compatibility, and version negotiation. TRIGGER when: designing, changing, or discussing any external API, working with .baboon model files, updating data models, adding new fields or types, bumping model versions, running code generation, implementing conversions, writing database migrations, or discussing API versioning strategy.
- constructive-test-taxonomy: Multi-axis classification of tests (Intention × Encapsulation × Isolation) for evaluating test quality and steering test-writing decisions. Replaces fuzzy Unit/Integration/E2E vocabulary with precise tags and a maintenance-cost heuristic. TRIGGER when: writing new tests, reviewing a test suite, deciding what to test or at what level, discussing mocks vs. real dependencies, classifying tests, or arguing about test coverage or test-strategy tradeoffs.
- dual-tests: Pattern for testing code that touches external systems: write one abstract test suite against an interface, then run it against both the production adapter (real DB/API/FS) and a hand-written in-memory dummy. Prefers manual dummies over auto-generated mocks. TRIGGER when: designing code that depends on a database, HTTP API, message queue, filesystem, or other external system; choosing between mocks, fakes, or dummies; adding integration points; setting up test infrastructure; or when the `constructive-test-taxonomy` skill calls for moving Communication-tier tests toward Blackbox-Atomic.
- environment: Bubblewrap "yolo" sandbox escape workflow (exchange scripts for reaching $HOME, system paths, or files outside the sandbox) plus in-sandbox nix/direnv usage. TRIGGER only when $SMIND_SANDBOXED=1 — i.e. running inside the yolo sandbox and you need to reach something outside it. SKIP entirely when $SMIND_SANDBOXED is unset: you are unsandboxed, so write directly and ignore this skill.
- izumi: Best-practice playbook for the izumi Scala ecosystem — distage (DI), distage-framework (roles/RoleAppMain), distage-testkit (test harness with memoization), distage-framework-docker (test containers), logstage (structured logging), and BIO (bifunctor effect typeclasses). Covers idiomatic bindings, Activation/Axis switching, Lifecycle discipline, plugin layout, and how to apply the `dual-tests` pattern inside distage so one test body runs against both a dummy and a real-DB adapter. TRIGGER when: writing or reviewing Scala code that imports `izumi.distage.*`, `izumi.logstage.*`, `izumi.functional.bio.*`, `distage.*`, `logstage.*`; designing a new distage plugin or Role; wiring a testkit `SpecZIO`/`Spec2`/`Spec1`; configuring `ContainerDef`/`DockerSupportModule`; choosing between `StandardAxis.Repo` or `Scene` tags; or implementing the dual-tests pattern in a distage project. The canonical reference project is https://github.com/7mind/distage-example.
- resilient-ws-ui: Guidelines for building applications that maintain reliable WebSocket connections to a backend and surface connection health clearly to the user. Invoke ONLY when the user explicitly references this skill by name (e.g. "/resilient-ws-ui", "use the resilient-ws-ui skill"). Do NOT auto-invoke based on topic match.
- tass: Target/Actual State Separation (TASS) pattern for reliable state management in event-driven control systems. TRIGGER when: implementing device or entity state management, designing control systems, working with target vs actual state separation, or building event-driven systems with commanded/observed state.
- cq:advance: Advance the WHOLE flow one cycle — chain /cq:investigate:advance, /cq:plan:advance, /cq:implement:advance to quiescence, then report DRAINED / BLOCKED-ON-QUESTIONS / MIXED.
- cq:implement-review: Implement-flow adversarial per-task review rubric + stdout-JSON contract, as a portable promptTemplate for non-Claude harnesses (pi/Codex). Symmetric to cq:plan-review. Judges ONE task's implementation, splits findings into autonomously-fixable criticism[], user-only questions[], and out-of-scope defects[], and emits the verdict as JSON on stdout. Writes NOTHING to the ledger — the orchestrator records the terminal review.
- cq:implement:advance: Advance an implement-flow run one full pass — pick DAG-ready tasks, implement each in an isolated worktree, adversarially review, autonomously fix criticism, register user questions, and merge back in dependency order.
- cq:implement:start: Start an implement-flow run — resolve the target milestones (default: all open ones), validate their task DAG, then hand off to the /cq:implement:advance loop.
- cq:investigate: Start an investigate-flow run — defect intake + bootstrap, then run the /cq:investigate:advance pass inline.
- cq:investigate:advance: Advance an investigate-flow run one research round — read defect state, form/extend the hypothesis tree, dispatch read-only explorers, validate every citation against source, adjudicate node status, and on a confirmed root cause file-and-defer the fix to plan-flow.
- cq:plan: Start a plan-flow goal — create the goal (from free text OR one-or-more idea-ids, one goal per idea), then hand off to the planner for the first clarifying questions.
- cq:plan-review: Shared adversarial plan-review rubric — judge an emitted plan for a goal against the fine-grained / sequenced / testable / grounded / complete rubric, classify findings into new_questions / criticism / defects, and emit a single fenced-json verdict on stdout. The CANONICAL rubric source; Claude's plan-reviewer agent and every non-Claude (Codex/Pi) reviewer judge the same way and emit the same JSON.
- cq:plan:advance: Advance plan-flow goals one full round — a given goal, or (no argument) every unlocked goal — running the planner↔reviewer loop until each needs the user or reaches `planned`.
- cq:plan:follow-up: Add new scope to an EXISTING plan-flow goal — append the follow-up request, re-open the goal, and hand to the planner for a fresh clarifying round.
- cq:planners: Session-only planner-set override — parse a natural-language planner instruction (e.g. "use grok and opus only") into canonical harness:model tokens via the cq.toml [aliases] table (from the ledger MCP get_planners/get_config) plus a documented fallback map; echo the resolved active set; confirm the override is SESSION-ONLY and reverts on the next fresh run.
- cq:reviewers: Session-only reviewer-set override — parse a natural-language reviewer instruction (e.g. "use grok and opus only") into canonical harness:model tokens via the cq.toml [aliases] table (from the ledger MCP get_reviewers/get_config) plus a documented fallback map; echo the resolved active set; confirm the override is SESSION-ONLY and reverts on the next fresh run.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- codex:rescue: Delegate investigation, an explicit fix request, or follow-up rescue work to the Codex rescue subagent
- codex:setup: Check whether the local Codex CLI is ready and optionally toggle the stop-time review gate
- codex:codex-cli-runtime: Internal helper contract for calling the codex-companion runtime from Claude Code
- codex:codex-result-handling: Internal guidance for presenting Codex helper output back to the user
- codex:gpt-5-4-prompting: Internal guidance for composing Codex and GPT-5.4 prompts for coding, review, diagnosis, and research tasks inside the Codex Claude Code plugin
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

1	//go:build realhosts
2	
3	package realhosts
4	
5	import (
6		"context"
7		"encoding/json"
8		"fmt"
9		"strings"
10		"testing"
11		"time"
12	)
13	
14	// DNS dial-by-name overlay addressing/wire parameters (Q36, stretch). Distinct inner
15	// addresses, port, and systemd units from the smoke/soak/failover tiers so a run of
16	// several tiers back to back never collides on a leftover interface or unit.
17	const (
18		dnsEdgeInner  = "10.10.1.1"
19		dnsConcInner  = "10.10.1.2"
20		dnsListenPort = 51840
21	
22		dnsConcUnit = "wanbond-dns-conc"
23		dnsEdgeUnit = "wanbond-dns-edge"
24	
25		// dnsBogusHost is a syntactically valid (passes config.validateHostname) but
26		// deliberately unresolvable hostname under the IANA-reserved "example" TLD
27		// (RFC 2606 guarantees it is never delegated), so the induced failure this
28		// subtest demonstrates is deterministic and touches no real DNS
29		// infrastructure.
30		dnsBogusHost = "this-host-does-not-exist.invalid.wanbond-dns-test.example"
31	)
32	
33	// Timeouts specific to this tier. dnsHandshakeTimeout is generous (a real resolver
34	// round trip plus the real NAT path) but the tier waits at most this long before
35	// REPORTING — never failing — a stalled resolution/handshake.
36	const (
37		dnsHandshakeTimeout = 45 * time.Second
38		dnsTrafficTimeout   = 20 * time.Second
39	)
40	
41	// TestRealDNSDialByName is the report-only dial-by-name stretch tier (Q36, M10/Q12
42	// discipline): it brings up the standing two-host testbed with the EDGE dialing the
43	// concentrator by its real DNS name (cfg.Conc.Addr — defaultConcHost, o3.7mind.io,
44	// which resolves to the standing ConcPubIP) with the peer's `dns = true` opt-in
45	// enabled, then repeats the same bring-up against a deliberately bogus hostname.
46	// Both scenarios REPORT their outcome (resolved address, time-to-first-handshake,
47	// and a steady-traffic sample) and NEVER fail the suite on that outcome — only the
48	// SSH/provisioning/build plumbing (a precondition, not the thing under test) can
49	// fail the test, mirroring every other tier in this package. There is no mid-session
50	// IP change: the concentrator's public IP is the fixed, standing ConcPubIP; this
51	// tier only proves the resolve-then-dial path against a real resolver and real NAT.
52	func TestRealDNSDialByName(t *testing.T) {
53		cfg := LoadConfig()
54		r := NewRunner(cfg)
55	
56		t.Logf("realhosts config: edge=%s concentrator=%s conc-public-ip=%s ssh-key=%s",
57			cfg.Edge.target(), cfg.Conc.target(), cfg.ConcPubIP, cfg.SSHKey)
58	
59		provision(t, r, cfg.Edge, ProvisionOpts{})
60		provision(t, r, cfg.Conc, ProvisionOpts{TunnelIface: tunnelIface})
61	
62		root := repoRoot(t)
63		syncAndBuild(t, r, cfg.Edge, root)
64		t.Cleanup(func() { removeRemoteDir(t, r, cfg.Edge) })
65		syncAndBuild(t, r, cfg.Conc, root)
66		t.Cleanup(func() { removeRemoteDir(t, r, cfg.Conc) })
67	
68		edgePriv, edgePub := genSmokeKey(t)
69		concPriv, concPub := genSmokeKey(t)
70		psk := randSmokeKey(t)
71	
72		edgeSrc := primaryIP(t, r, cfg.Edge)
73		concSrc := primaryIP(t, r, cfg.Conc)
74		t.Logf("path source addrs: edge=%s concentrator=%s", edgeSrc, concSrc)
75	
76		// The concentrator listens on the real public endpoint (ConcPubIP:dnsListenPort)
77		// throughout BOTH subtests; only the edge's configured peer HOSTNAME changes
78		// between them, so one concentrator bring-up and one iperf3 server suffice.
79		startDNSConcentrator(t, r, cfg.Conc, concSrc, concPriv, edgePub, psk)
80		startIperfServer(t, r, cfg.Conc, dnsConcInner)
81	
82		t.Run("valid_name", func(t *testing.T) {
83			runDNSDialScenario(t, r, cfg.Edge, cfg.Conc.Addr, edgeSrc, edgePriv, concPub, psk)
84		})
85		t.Run("bogus_name", func(t *testing.T) {
86			runDNSDialScenario(t, r, cfg.Edge, dnsBogusHost, edgeSrc, edgePriv, concPub, psk)
87		})
88	}
89	
90	// startDNSConcentrator writes and starts the concentrator's dial-by-name-tier
91	// daemon (own config/unit/port, distinct from the smoke tier's), then waits for its
92	// TUN and addresses it. This is INFRASTRUCTURE the scenario is built on, not the
93	// dial-by-name outcome under test, so — matching the rest of this package — it
94	// Fatals on failure.
95	func startDNSConcentrator(t *testing.T, r *Runner, conc Host, concSrc, concPriv, edgePub, psk string) {
96		t.Helper()
97	
98		concCfg := fmt.Sprintf(`role = "concentrator"
99	psk = "%s"
100	
101	[[paths]]
102	name = "wan0"
103	source_addr = "%s"
104	
105	[wireguard]
106	private_key = "%s"
107	listen_port = %d
108	
109	[[wireguard.peers]]
110	public_key = "%s"
111	allowed_ips = ["%s/32"]
112	
113	[log]
114	level = "info"
115	`, psk, concSrc, concPriv, dnsListenPort, edgePub, dnsEdgeInner)
116	
117		concCfgPath := smokeRemoteDir + "/dns-conc.toml"
118		writeRemoteFile(t, r, conc, concCfgPath, concCfg)
119	
120		stopUnit(t, r, conc, dnsConcUnit)
121		delLink(t, r, conc, tunnelIface)
122		t.Cleanup(func() {
123			stopUnit(t, r, conc, dnsConcUnit)
124			delLink(t, r, conc, tunnelIface)
125		})
126	
127		ctx, cancel := context.WithTimeout(context.Background(), smokeSSHTimeout)
128		defer cancel()
129		start := fmt.Sprintf("sudo systemd-run --unit=%s --service-type=simple %s --config %s",
130			dnsConcUnit, smokeBin, concCfgPath)
131		if _, err := r.Run(ctx, conc, start); err != nil {
132			t.Fatalf("concentrator: start wanbond daemon failed: %v", err)
133		}
134		t.Logf("concentrator: wanbond daemon started (unit=%s)", dnsConcUnit)
135	
136		if !waitRemoteLink(t, r, conc, tunnelIface, linkAppearTimeout) {
137			dumpUnitLog(t, r, conc, dnsConcUnit)
138			t.Fatalf("concentrator: %s never appeared", tunnelIface)
139		}
140		addressLink(t, r, conc, dnsConcInner)
141	}
142	
143	// runDNSDialScenario writes and starts the EDGE daemon dialing dialHost:dnsListenPort
144	// (the peer's `dns = true` opt-in enabled) against the already-running concentrator,
145	// then observes — but does NOT gate on — the resolve-then-dial outcome: the resolved
146	// address the daemon's own re-resolution controller installed (read from ITS journal,
147	// not a client-side lookup, so this proves wanbond's own resolver path rather than the
148	// test host's), the time-to-first-handshake, and a short best-effort traffic sample
149	// taken only once a handshake completed.
150	//
151	// Standing up the daemon and its TUN is a PRECONDITION (Fatal on failure, matching the
152	// rest of this package); the RESOLUTION / HANDSHAKE / TRAFFIC outcome itself never
153	// calls t.Fatalf/t.Errorf — only t.Logf. This is the acceptance property the
154	// "bogus_name" subtest exists to demonstrate: an unresolvable hostname reports a
155	// failed resolution/handshake/traffic outcome and the subtest still PASSES.
156	func runDNSDialScenario(t *testing.T, r *Runner, edge Host, dialHost, edgeSrc, edgePriv, concPub, psk string) {
157		t.Helper()
158	
159		edgeCfg := fmt.Sprintf(`role = "edge"
160	psk = "%s"
161	
162	[[paths]]
163	name = "wan0"
164	source_addr = "%s"
165	
166	[wireguard]
167	private_key = "%s"
168

179		edgeCfgPath := smokeRemoteDir + "/dns-edge.toml"

181	
182		stopUnit(t, r, edge, dnsEdgeUnit)
183		delLink(t, r, edge, tunnelIface)
184		t.Cleanup(func() {
185			stopUnit(t, r, edge, dnsEdgeUnit)
186			delLink(t, r, edge, tunnelIface)
187		})
188	
189		startAt := edgeClockNow(t, r, edge)
190	
191		ctx, cancel := context.WithTimeout(context.Background(), smokeSSHTimeout)
192		start := fmt.Sprintf("sudo systemd-run --unit=%s --service-type=simple %s --config %s",
193			dnsEdgeUnit, smokeBin, edgeCfgPath)
194		_, err := r.Run(ctx, edge, start)
195		cancel()
196		if err != nil {
197			t.Fatalf("edge: start wanbond daemon (dial %q) failed: %v", dialHost, err)
198		}
199		t.Logf("edge: wanbond daemon started dialing %q (unit=%s)", dialHost, dnsEdgeUnit)
200	
201		if !waitRemoteLink(t, r, edge, tunnelIface, linkAppearTimeout) {
202			dumpUnitLog(t, r, edge, dnsEdgeUnit)
203			t.Fatalf("edge: %s never appeared", tunnelIface)
204		}
205		addressLink(t, r, edge, dnsEdgeInner)
206	
207		// --- Resolution + handshake (REPORT-ONLY: never fails the suite) -------------------
208		handshakeOK := pingUntil(t, r, edge, dnsConcInner, dnsHandshakeTimeout)
209		var handshakeElapsed time.Duration
210		if handshakeOK {
211			handshakeElapsed = edgeClockNow(t, r, edge).Sub(startAt)
212		}
213	
214		journal := readDaemonJournal(t, r, edge, dnsEdgeUnit)
215		resolvedAddr, resolveElapsed, resolveOK := firstResolutionAfter(journal, startAt)
216	
217		if resolveOK {
218			t.Logf("RESOLUTION: %q -> %s (%s after daemon start)", dialHost, resolvedAddr, resolveElapsed)
219		} else {
220			t.Logf("RESOLUTION: %q did not resolve to a usable endpoint within the window", dialHost)
221		}
222		if handshakeOK {
223			t.Logf("HANDSHAKE: OK (time-to-first-handshake ~ %s)", handshakeElapsed.Round(time.Millisecond))
224		} else {
225			t.Logf("HANDSHAKE: did not complete within %s", dnsHandshakeTimeout)
226		}
227	
228		// --- Steady traffic (REPORT-ONLY; skipped entirely when there was no handshake) ----
229		var mbps float64
230		trafficOK := false
231		if handshakeOK {
232			mbps, trafficOK = dnsTrafficSample(t, r, edge, dnsConcInner)
233		}
234		if trafficOK {
235			t.Logf("TRAFFIC: %.2f Mbit/s over the dial-by-name tunnel", mbps)
236		} else {
237			t.Logf("TRAFFIC: no sample (handshake=%t)", handshakeOK)
238		}
239	
240		// Final report block (report-only; nothing above or below gates the subtest).
241		t.Logf("=== DNS DIAL-BY-NAME RESULT ===\n"+
242			"  dial host:       %s\n"+
243			"  resolved:        %t (%s)\n"+
244			"  handshake:       %t (%s)\n"+
245			"  traffic sample:  %t (%.2f Mbit/s)",
246			dialHost, resolveOK, resolvedAddr, handshakeOK, handshakeElapsed.Round(time.Millisecond), trafficOK, mbps)
247	}

249	// dumpUnitLog logs the tail of unit's journal on host for diagnosing a failed
250	// bring-up. A parameterized sibling of dumpDaemonLog (which hardcodes smokeUnit),
251	// needed here because this tier's daemons run under their own unit names.
252	func dumpUnitLog(t *testing.T, r *Runner, host Host, unit string) {
253		t.Helper()
254		ctx, cancel := context.WithTimeout(context.Background(), smokeSSHTimeout)
255		defer cancel()
256		res, err := r.Run(ctx, host, "sudo journalctl -u "+unit+" --no-pager -n 50 2>&1; true")
257		if err != nil {
258			t.Logf("%s: could not read %s journal: %v", host.Role, unit, err)
259			return
260		}
261		t.Logf("%s: %s journal (last 50 lines):\n%s", host.Role, unit, res.Stdout)
262	}
263	
264	// dnsResolutionRecord is the subset of the "hub failover: first endpoint resolution"
265	// slog line (internal/device/failover.go) this tier reads: it is logged exactly once,
266	// the moment the re-resolution controller installs the FIRST successfully resolved
267	// address on the engine peer.
268	type dnsResolutionRecord struct {
269		Time       time.Time `json:"time"`
270		Msg        string    `json:"msg"`
271		ToEndpoint string    `json:"to_endpoint"`
272	}
273	
274	// firstResolutionAfter scans journal for the "first endpoint resolution" transition
275	// logged strictly after `after` and returns the address it installed and the elapsed
276	// time from `after` to that transition. ok is false when no such transition appears
277	// — i.e. the hostname never resolved to a usable address in the window, which is
278	// exactly what the bogus_name subtest expects to observe and report.
279	func firstResolutionAfter(journal string, after time.Time) (endpoint string, elapsed time.Duration, ok bool) {
280		for _, line := range strings.Split(journal, "\n") {
281			if !strings.Contains(line, "first endpoint resolution") {
282				continue
283			}
284			var rec dnsResolutionRecord
285			if err := json.Unmarshal([]byte(line), &rec); err != nil {
286				continue
287			}
288			if rec.ToEndpoint == "" || !rec.Time.After(after) {
289				continue
290			}
291			return rec.ToEndpoint, rec.Time.Sub(after), true
292		}
293		return "", 0, false
294	}
295	
296	// dnsTrafficSample runs a short best-effort iperf3 TCP sample from edge to the
297	// concentrator's inner IP and reports the achieved throughput. Unlike iperfTCP
298	// (smoke tier), every failure is LOGGED and returns ok=false rather than
299	// t.Fatalf — the whole point of this tier is that a data-plane sample taken after a
300	// bogus-name dial REPORTS, it never fails the suite.
301	func dnsTrafficSample(t *testing.T, r *Runner, edge Host, serverIP string) (mbps float64, ok bool) {
302		t.Helper()
303		ctx, cancel := context.WithTimeout(context.Background(), dnsTrafficTimeout)
304		defer cancel()
305		res, err := r.Run(ctx, edge, fmt.Sprintf("iperf3 -c %s -t 5 -J", serverIP))
306		if err != nil {
307			t.Logf("edge: dial-by-name traffic sample failed: %v", err)
308			return 0, false
309		}
310		var rep iperfReport
311		if err := json.Unmarshal([]byte(res.Stdout), &rep); err != nil {
312			t.Logf("edge: parse dial-by-name iperf3 JSON failed: %v\n%s", err, res.Stdout)
313			return 0, false
314		}
315		return rep.End.SumSent.BitsPerSecond / 1e6, true
316	}

1	package device
2	
3	import (
4		"strconv"
5		"strings"
6		"sync"
7		"time"
8	
9		awgdevice "github.com/amnezia-vpn/amneziawg-go/device"
10	
11		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/log"
12		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/metrics"
13		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/telemetry"
14	)
15	
16	// sessionPollInterval is the cadence at which the session monitor reads the engine's
17	// last-handshake state to drive the 0->1 'session established' edge log. It matches the
18	// probe cadence (the same rate the liveness plane runs at) so the session-established
19	// transition is timestamped close to when it happens — the /metrics gauge itself is
20	// read independently at scrape time and needs no polling.
21	const sessionPollInterval = telemetry.DefaultProbeInterval
22	
23	// ipcGetter is the read seam onto the amneziawg engine's UAPI GET: it yields the
24	// newline-delimited peer state string that carries each peer's last_handshake_time_sec /
25	// _nsec lines. *awgdevice.Device satisfies it; a fake satisfies it in unit tests so the
26	// snapshot parsing and the 0->1 edge run without a live engine.
27	type ipcGetter interface {
28		IpcGet() (string, error)
29	}
30	
31	// sessionSnapshotter yields the current WG-session snapshot the metrics adapter exposes
32	// and the edge detector polls. *sessionMonitor satisfies it against the live engine; a
33	// fake satisfies it in unit tests.
34	type sessionSnapshotter interface {
35		SessionSnapshot() metrics.SessionSnapshot
36	}
37	
38	// sessionMonitor reads the amneziawg engine's peer last-handshake state at scrape/poll
39	// time and resolves it into a metrics.SessionSnapshot. It is the ONLY WG-session coupling
40	// the metrics plane takes; the bind stays WG-unaware. The engine is read through the UAPI
41	// GET seam (there is no public accessor for a peer's last-handshake instant), so the read
42	// is a parse of the peer dump rather than a field load — acceptable at scrape/probe
43	// cadence for the edge's single peer. It is stateless (no edge memory): the current
44	// verdict is a pure function of the engine state and the clock, so it is safe for the
45	// concurrent scrape goroutine and the poll loop to share one instance.
46	type sessionMonitor struct {
47		engine ipcGetter
48		clock  telemetry.Clock
49		// expiry is the session-validity window: a completed handshake older than this no
50		// longer counts as established (the tunnel is wedged, not converged). It is WireGuard's
51		// RejectAfterTime — the point past which the current keypair is dead — so a healthy
52		// tunnel (which rekeys well within it, and keeps the edge session warm via keepalive)
53		// never flaps to 0, while a tunnel whose handshake stops advancing does.
54		expiry time.Duration
55	}
56	
57	// newSessionMonitor builds a session monitor over the engine. The clock is injected so the
58	// age/freshness derivation is deterministic under test.
59	func newSessionMonitor(engine ipcGetter, clock telemetry.Clock) *sessionMonitor {
60		return &sessionMonitor{engine: engine, clock: clock, expiry: awgdevice.RejectAfterTime}
61	}
62	
63	// SessionSnapshot reads the engine and resolves the connection-scoped WG-session verdict:
64	// the MOST RECENT completed handshake across all configured peers (for the edge's single
65	// concentrator peer this is simply that peer's handshake; for a multi-peer concentrator it
66	// is "at least one edge session is live"). Established is true only when such a handshake
67	// exists AND its age is within the validity window. An engine read error, or no completed
68	// handshake on any peer, yields the zero snapshot (Established=false, age=0) — the "still
69	// converging" reading. A negative age (clock skew) is clamped to zero.
70	func (m *sessionMonitor) SessionSnapshot() metrics.SessionSnapshot {
71		dump, err := m.engine.IpcGet()
72		if err != nil {
73			return metrics.SessionSnapshot{}
74		}
75		latest := latestHandshakeNano(dump)
76		if latest == 0 {
77			return metrics.SessionSnapshot{}
78		}
79		age := m.clock.Now().Sub(time.Unix(0, latest))
80		if age < 0 {
81			age = 0
82		}
83		return metrics.SessionSnapshot{
84			Established:      age <= m.expiry,
85			LastHandshakeAge: age,
86		}
87	}
88	
89	// latestHandshakeNano parses an amneziawg UAPI GET dump and returns the most recent peer
90	// handshake instant as Unix nanoseconds, or 0 when no peer carries a completed handshake.
91	// Each peer block emits `last_handshake_time_sec=<s>` immediately followed by
92	// `last_handshake_time_nsec=<ns>`; a never-handshaked peer emits 0/0. The two lines are
93	// recombined into a single nanosecond instant, and the maximum across peers is returned.
94	func latestHandshakeNano(dump string) int64 {
95		var latest int64
96		var sec int64
97		var haveSec bool
98		for _, line := range strings.Split(dump, "\n") {
99			key, val, ok := strings.Cut(strings.TrimSpace(line), "=")
100			if !ok {
101				continue
102			}
103			switch key {
104			case "last_handshake_time_sec":
105				s, err := strconv.ParseInt(val, 10, 64)
106				if err != nil {
107					haveSec = false
108					continue
109				}
110				sec, haveSec = s, true
111			case "last_handshake_time_nsec":
112				if !haveSec {
113					continue
114				}
115				haveSec = false
116				ns, err := strconv.ParseInt(val, 10, 64)
117				if err != nil {
118					continue
119				}
120				nano := sec*int64(time.Second) + ns
121				if nano > latest {
122					latest = nano
123				}
124			}
125		}
126		return latest
127	}
128	
129	// sessionEdge detects the 0->1 (false->true) WG-session-established transition. observe is
130	// called on each poll with the current established verdict; it returns true EXACTLY on a
131	// false->true edge, so the caller emits the 'session established' record once per newly
132	// (re)established session. A steady-state 1->1 poll returns false (no duplicate log); a
133	// 1->0->1 sequence (a session lost and re-established) fires again on the fresh rise.
134	type sessionEdge struct {
135		established bool
136	}
137	
138	func (e *sessionEdge) observe(established bool) (rose bool) {
139		rose = established && !e.established
140		e.established = established

constructor over an injected `dnsresolve.Resolver`, the failover controller it
drives, a `telemetry.Clock`, and the `[dns]` poll interval + per-lookup timeout) and
runs its own device-lifecycle loop **off the send hot path** — all lookups happen on
its goroutine; results are applied only through `updateResolution`. Each evaluation:

- **Poll** every hostname spec on the fixed `[dns]` cadence; on a **successful,
  non-empty** lookup the addrs are **family-filtered then ordered** — addrs of a
  family no local path can source (a path binds a socket whose family is fixed by its
  `source_addr`, so an AAAA answer on a v4-only edge is unreachable and is dropped),
  then IPv4 first, then IPv6, deduped — a deterministic order so an unchanged answer
  yields a byte-identical expansion. The result is handed to `updateResolution`, which
  **repoints only on an actual active-IP change** (D32 no-op suppression). When the
  transport exposes a TTL (DoH/DoT), the next poll is clamped to
  `min(pollInterval, minTTL)`.
- **Liveness-loss trigger**: the instant every path to the **active** endpoint reads
  `StateDown` — the *same* `allDown` sweep the failover loop advances on (Q34: the
  two controllers coordinate purely through the shared lock and the update API) — the
  active spec is re-resolved **out of band**, edge-triggered, without waiting for the
  next poll tick. This out-of-band re-arm is clamped to `min(pollInterval, minTTL)`
  too, so record freshness holds on exactly the hub-loss path where it matters most.
- **Retention invariant (D46)**: a lookup that **fails** (error/timeout/NXDOMAIN) or
  yields an **empty** usable set — including an answer that **filters down to empty**
  because it carries no family any local path can source — **never publishes**: the
  spec keeps its last-good expansion and the controller retries next tick. A transient
  resolver fault, or an answer for a family the edge cannot reach, therefore never
  tears down a working endpoint set, and `hubFailover` never sees a previously-resolved
  active spec collapse to empty (the condition its `total < 2` guard could otherwise
  strand the bond on).

This controller runs **even for a single-hostname peer** (to track a changing DDNS
address), independent of hub-failover's `>= 2` guard; the first successful resolution
of a hostname-only peer is what boot-adopts its active endpoint.

**Device lifecycle** (T74). At `Up` the device does one **bounded initial resolve**
of each hostname spec (the `[dns]` per-lookup timeout) and builds the engine/UAPI peer
endpoint **only from resolved entries** — the flattened head of the seeded specs. If a
name does not resolve in the boot window (single-hostname peer, resolver down), the
tunnel comes up **without a peer endpoint** (tolerant boot, Q30 defer-and-reconcile —
an unresolvable name never hard-fails bring-up); the concentrator already runs
endpoint-less, so the engine supports it. The resolver is constructed **once**, and
**only when some peer carries a hostname spec** — a zero-hostname config builds no
resolver and starts no loop (Q29 inertness). The **first-resolve install path (R70)**
is load-bearing: `SetPeerRemote` repoints the bind's per-path remotes but **never sets
the engine peer's endpoint**, which is populated **only** by a UAPI `endpoint=` line
routed through `Multipath.ParseEndpoint`. So after an endpoint-less boot, the first
successful resolve must **install** the resolved endpoint on the engine peer via the
UAPI/IpcSet path (`deviceInstallEndpoint`) **then** re-handshake — the initiation now
has an addressable endpoint. Subsequent re-resolves of an already-installed peer take
the normal `SetPeerRemote` repoint path (the engine's virtual endpoint stays stable per
A1; only the bind remotes move). The re-resolution loop's stopper is held on the
`Tunnel` and invoked by `Close` between the hub-failover stop and the engine teardown.
The whole flow — endpoint-less boot while the name is unresolvable, the R70
first-resolve install, a mid-session concentrator-IP change, and the re-resolve repoint
whose `SetPeerRemote` re-baselines the receive resequencer so post-change traffic
actually resumes (the D32 guard) — is validated end to end by the privileged netns e2e
`TestDNSHubResolveAndReroute` (Q36), with a hermetic in-namespace UDP DNS responder as
the sole answer source (no external DNS egress).

### Per-path telemetry — `internal/telemetry`

Measures per-path quality (RTT, loss, jitter) by exchanging authenticated PROBE
frames, and drives liveness/failover decisions. Carries the anti-replay
primitives (`AntiReplay` high-water, `ControlGuard`) that protect PROBE and
CONTROL against replay of captured valid-MAC frames.

### Receive resequencer — `internal/reseq`

Bonding across paths of different latency reorders packets. The resequencer holds
a **bounded window** with a timeout and restores order **before** the inner
WireGuard anti-replay window sees the traffic — critical, because WG would
otherwise drop legitimately-reordered datagrams. It runs its **own outer sequence
space** and never touches the inner WireGuard counter (a core invariant).

### FEC — `internal/fec` + `internal/adaptivefec`

- `fec` implements Reed-Solomon over groups of *K* data shards + *M* parity
  shards (via `klauspost/reedsolomon`). Lost DATA frames are reconstructed from
  PARITY without retransmission.
- `adaptivefec` is a pure, deterministic control loop that floats *M* in
  `[0, ceiling]` to track measured loss. It can be driven by a **`target_residual`
  SLA** (invert the binomial residual `E[max(0,D−M)]/K` to the smallest *M*
  meeting the target) or a legacy `safety_factor` multiplier (mutually
  exclusive). Both off by default; enable with `[fec] enabled = true`
  (+ `adaptive = true`).

> **Dependency invariant (pinned in `go.mod`):** the adaptive path codes each
> group `RS(m, k≤ceiling)` yet decodes against a single `RS(m, ceiling)` codec.
> That is byte-exact only because reedsolomon's default matrix makes parity shard
> *j* identical across total-parity counts — an *undocumented* property. Any
> reedsolomon bump must be re-verified against
> `TestKlauspostParityPrefixStableInvariant` (`internal/fec`) before landing.

### Supporting packages

- `internal/config` — loads the single TOML config, validates fail-fast at load
  (0600 perms, complete-or-absent amnezia block, FEC bounds, scheduler
  invariants, unique `source_addr`). The optional `[dns]` block selects the
  resolver transport (system default, DoH, or DoT) a peer's opt-in hostname
  endpoint is resolved through, enforcing the BOOTSTRAP-IP invariant (a
  hostname-form `doh_url`/`dot_server` requires an explicit `bootstrap_ip`;
  an IP-literal host rejects a non-empty `bootstrap_ip` as a mode mismatch)

14	// DNS dial-by-name overlay addressing/wire parameters (Q36, stretch). Distinct inner
15	// addresses, port, and systemd units from the smoke/soak/failover tiers so a run of
16	// several tiers back to back never collides on a leftover interface or unit.
17	const (
18		dnsEdgeInner  = "10.10.1.1"
19		dnsConcInner  = "10.10.1.2"
20		dnsListenPort = 51840
21	
22		dnsConcUnit = "wanbond-dns-conc"
23		dnsEdgeUnit = "wanbond-dns-edge"
24	
25		// dnsBogusHost is a syntactically valid (passes config.validateHostname) but
26		// deliberately unresolvable hostname under the IANA-reserved "example" TLD
27		// (RFC 2606 guarantees it is never delegated), so the induced failure this
28		// subtest demonstrates is deterministic and touches no real DNS
29		// infrastructure.
30		dnsBogusHost = "this-host-does-not-exist.invalid.wanbond-dns-test.example"
31	)
32	
33	// Timeouts specific to this tier. dnsHandshakeTimeout is generous (a real resolver
34	// round trip plus the real NAT path) but the tier waits at most this long before
35	// REPORTING — never failing — a stalled resolution/handshake.
36	const (
37		dnsHandshakeTimeout = 45 * time.Second
38		dnsTrafficTimeout   = 20 * time.Second
39	)
40	
41	// TestRealDNSDialByName is the report-only dial-by-name stretch tier (Q36, M10/Q12
42	// discipline): it brings up the standing two-host testbed with the EDGE dialing the
43	// concentrator by its real DNS name (cfg.Conc.Addr — defaultConcHost, o3.7mind.io,
44	// which resolves to the standing ConcPubIP) with the peer's `dns = true` opt-in
45	// enabled, then repeats the same bring-up against a deliberately bogus hostname.
46	// Both scenarios REPORT their outcome (resolved address, time-to-first-handshake,
47	// and a steady-traffic sample) and NEVER fail the suite on that outcome — only the
48	// SSH/provisioning/build plumbing (a precondition, not the thing under test) can
49	// fail the test, mirroring every other tier in this package. There is no mid-session
50	// IP change: the concentrator's public IP is the fixed, standing ConcPubIP; this
51	// tier only proves the resolve-then-dial path against a real resolver and real NAT.
52	func TestRealDNSDialByName(t *testing.T) {
53		cfg := LoadConfig()
54		r := NewRunner(cfg)
55	
56		t.Logf("realhosts config: edge=%s concentrator=%s conc-public-ip=%s ssh-key=%s",
57			cfg.Edge.target(), cfg.Conc.target(), cfg.ConcPubIP, cfg.SSHKey)
58	
59		provision(t, r, cfg.Edge, ProvisionOpts{})
60		provision(t, r, cfg.Conc, ProvisionOpts{TunnelIface: tunnelIface})
61	
62		root := repoRoot(t)
63		syncAndBuild(t, r, cfg.Edge, root)
64		t.Cleanup(func() { removeRemoteDir(t, r, cfg.Edge) })
65		syncAndBuild(t, r, cfg.Conc, root)
66		t.Cleanup(func() { removeRemoteDir(t, r, cfg.Conc) })
67	
68		edgePriv, edgePub := genSmokeKey(t)
69		concPriv, concPub := genSmokeKey(t)
70		psk := randSmokeKey(t)
71	
72		edgeSrc := primaryIP(t, r, cfg.Edge)
73		concSrc := primaryIP(t, r, cfg.Conc)
74		t.Logf("path source addrs: edge=%s concentrator=%s", edgeSrc, concSrc)
75	
76		// The concentrator listens on the real public endpoint (ConcPubIP:dnsListenPort)
77		// throughout BOTH subtests; only the edge's configured peer HOSTNAME changes
78		// between them, so one concentrator bring-up and one iperf3 server suffice.
79		startDNSConcentrator(t, r, cfg.Conc, concSrc, concPriv, edgePub, psk)
80		startIperfServer(t, r, cfg.Conc, dnsConcInner)
81	
82		t.Run("valid_name", func(t *testing.T) {
83			runDNSDialScenario(t, r, cfg.Edge, cfg.Conc.Addr, edgeSrc, edgePriv, concPub, psk)
84		})
85		t.Run("bogus_name", func(t *testing.T) {
86			runDNSDialScenario(t, r, cfg.Edge, dnsBogusHost, edgeSrc, edgePriv, concPub, psk)
87		})
88	}
89	
90	// startDNSConcentrator writes and starts the concentrator's dial-by-name-tier
91	// daemon (own config/unit/port, distinct from the smoke tier's), then waits for its
92	// TUN and addresses it. This is INFRASTRUCTURE the scenario is built on, not the
93	// dial-by-name outcome under test, so — matching the rest of this package — it
94	// Fatals on failure.
95	func startDNSConcentrator(t *testing.T, r *Runner, conc Host, concSrc, concPriv, edgePub, psk string) {
96		t.Helper()
97	
98		concCfg := fmt.Sprintf(`role = "concentrator"
99	psk = "%s"
100	
101	[[paths]]
102	name = "wan0"
103	source_addr = "%s"
104	
105	[wireguard]
106	private_key = "%s"
107	listen_port = %d
108	
109	[[wireguard.peers]]
110	public_key = "%s"
111	allowed_ips = ["%s/32"]
112	
113	[log]
114	level = "info"
115	`, psk, concSrc, concPriv, dnsListenPort, edgePub, dnsEdgeInner)
116	
117		concCfgPath := smokeRemoteDir + "/dns-conc.toml"
118		writeRemoteFile(t, r, conc, concCfgPath, concCfg)
119	
120		stopUnit(t, r, conc, dnsConcUnit)
121		delLink(t, r, conc, tunnelIface)
122		t.Cleanup(func() {
123			stopUnit(t, r, conc, dnsConcUnit)
124			delLink(t, r, conc, tunnelIface)
125		})
126	
127		ctx, cancel := context.WithTimeout(context.Background(), smokeSSHTimeout)
128		defer cancel()
129		start := fmt.Sprintf("sudo systemd-run --unit=%s --service-type=simple %s --config %s",
130			dnsConcUnit, smokeBin, concCfgPath)
131		if _, err := r.Run(ctx, conc, start); err != nil {
132			t.Fatalf("concentrator: start wanbond daemon failed: %v", err)
133		}
134		t.Logf("concentrator: wanbond daemon started (unit=%s)", dnsConcUnit)
135	
136		if !waitRemoteLink(t, r, conc, tunnelIface, linkAppearTimeout) {
137			dumpUnitLog(t, r, conc, dnsConcUnit)
138			t.Fatalf("concentrator: %s never appeared", tunnelIface)
139		}
140		addressLink(t, r, conc, dnsConcInner)
141	}
142	
143	// runDNSDialScenario writes and starts the EDGE daemon dialing dialHost:dnsListenPort
144	// (the peer's `dns = true` opt-in enabled) against the already-running concentrator,
145	// then observes — but does NOT gate on — the resolve-then-dial outcome: the resolved
146	// address the daemon's own re-resolution controller installed (read from ITS journal,
147	// not a client-side lookup, so this proves wanbond's own resolver path rather than the
148	// test host's), the time-to-first-handshake, and a short best-effort traffic sample
149	// taken only once a handshake completed.
150	//
151	// Standing up the daemon and its TUN is a PRECONDITION (Fatal on failure, matching the
152	// rest of this package); the RESOLUTION / HANDSHAKE / TRAFFIC outcome itself never
153	// calls t.Fatalf/t.Errorf — only t.Logf. This is the acceptance property the
154	// "bogus_name" subtest exists to demonstrate: an unresolvable hostname reports a
155	// failed resolution/handshake/traffic outcome and the subtest still PASSES.
156	func runDNSDialScenario(t *testing.T, r *Runner, edge Host, dialHost, edgeSrc, edgePriv, concPub, psk string) {
157		t.Helper()
158	
159		edgeCfg := fmt.Sprintf(`role = "edge"
160	psk = "%s"
161	
162	[[paths]]
163	name = "wan0"
164	source_addr = "%s"
165	
166	[wireguard]
167	private_key = "%s"
168	
169	[[wireguard.peers]]
170	public_key = "%s"
171	endpoint = "%s:%d"
172	dns = true
173	allowed_ips = ["%s/32"]
174	
175	[log]
176	level = "info"
177	`, psk, edgeSrc, edgePriv, concPub, dialHost, dnsListenPort, dnsConcInner)
178	
179		edgeCfgPath := smokeRemoteDir + "/dns-edge.toml"
180		writeRemoteFile(t, r, edge, edgeCfgPath, edgeCfg)
181	
182		stopUnit(t, r, edge, dnsEdgeUnit)
183		delLink(t, r, edge, tunnelIface)
184		t.Cleanup(func() {
185			stopUnit(t, r, edge, dnsEdgeUnit)
186			delLink(t, r, edge, tunnelIface)
187		})
188	
189		startAt := edgeClockNow(t, r, edge)
190	
191		ctx, cancel := context.WithTimeout(context.Background(), smokeSSHTimeout)
192		start := fmt.Sprintf("sudo systemd-run --unit=%s --service-type=simple %s --config %s",
193			dnsEdgeUnit, smokeBin, edgeCfgPath)
194		_, err := r.Run(ctx, edge, start)
195		cancel()
196		if err != nil {
197			t.Fatalf("edge: start wanbond daemon (dial %q) failed: %v", dialHost, err)
198		}
199		t.Logf("edge: wanbond daemon started dialing %q (unit=%s)", dialHost, dnsEdgeUnit)
200	
201		if !waitRemoteLink(t, r, edge, tunnelIface, linkAppearTimeout) {
202			dumpUnitLog(t, r, edge, dnsEdgeUnit)
203			t.Fatalf("edge: %s never appeared", tunnelIface)
204		}
205		addressLink(t, r, edge, dnsEdgeInner)
206	
207		// --- Resolution + handshake (REPORT-ONLY: never fails the suite) -------------------
208		handshakeOK := pingUntil(t, r, edge, dnsConcInner, dnsHandshakeTimeout)
209		var handshakeElapsed time.Duration
210		if handshakeOK {
211			handshakeElapsed = edgeClockNow(t, r, edge).Sub(startAt)
212		}
213	
214		journal := readDaemonJournal(t, r, edge, dnsEdgeUnit)
215		resolvedAddr, resolveElapsed, resolveOK := firstResolutionAfter(journal, startAt)
216	
217		if resolveOK {
218			t.Logf("RESOLUTION: %q -> %s (%s after daemon start)", dialHost, resolvedAddr, resolveElapsed)
219		} else {
220			t.Logf("RESOLUTION: %q did not resolve to a usable endpoint within the window", dialHost)
221		}
222		if handshakeOK {
223			t.Logf("HANDSHAKE: OK (time-to-first-handshake ~ %s)", handshakeElapsed.Round(time.Millisecond))
224		} else {
225			t.Logf("HANDSHAKE: did not complete within %s", dnsHandshakeTimeout)
226		}
227	
228		// --- Steady traffic (REPORT-ONLY; skipped entirely when there was no handshake) ----
229		var mbps float64
230		trafficOK := false
231		if handshakeOK {
232			mbps, trafficOK = dnsTrafficSample(t, r, edge, dnsConcInner)
233		}
234		if trafficOK {
235			t.Logf("TRAFFIC: %.2f Mbit/s over the dial-by-name tunnel", mbps)
236		} else {
237			t.Logf("TRAFFIC: no sample (handshake=%t)", handshakeOK)
238		}
239	
240		// Final report block (report-only; nothing above or below gates the subtest).
241		t.Logf("=== DNS DIAL-BY-NAME RESULT ===\n"+
242			"  dial host:       %s\n"+
243			"  resolved:        %t (%s)\n"+
244			"  handshake:       %t (%s)\n"+
245			"  traffic sample:  %t (%.2f Mbit/s)",
246			dialHost, resolveOK, resolvedAddr, handshakeOK, handshakeElapsed.Round(time.Millisecond), trafficOK, mbps)
247	}
248	
249	// dumpUnitLog logs the tail of unit's journal on host for diagnosing a failed
250	// bring-up. A parameterized sibling of dumpDaemonLog (which hardcodes smokeUnit),
251	// needed here because this tier's daemons run under their own unit names.
252	func dumpUnitLog(t *testing.T, r *Runner, host Host, unit string) {
253		t.Helper()
254		ctx, cancel := context.WithTimeout(context.Background(), smokeSSHTimeout)
255		defer cancel()
256		res, err := r.Run(ctx, host, "sudo journalctl -u "+unit+" --no-pager -n 50 2>&1; true")
257		if err != nil {
258			t.Logf("%s: could not read %s journal: %v", host.Role, unit, err)
259			return
260		}
261		t.Logf("%s: %s journal (last 50 lines):\n%s", host.Role, unit, res.Stdout)
262	}
263	
264	// dnsResolutionRecord is the subset of the "hub failover: first endpoint resolution"
265	// slog line (internal/device/failover.go) this tier reads: it is logged exactly once,
266	// the moment the re-resolution controller installs the FIRST successfully resolved
267	// address on the engine peer.
268	type dnsResolutionRecord struct {
269		Time       time.Time `json:"time"`
270		Msg        string    `json:"msg"`
271		ToEndpoint string    `json:"to_endpoint"`
272	}
273	
274	// firstResolutionAfter scans journal for the "first endpoint resolution" transition
275	// logged strictly after `after` and returns the address it installed and the elapsed
276	// time from `after` to that transition. ok is false when no such transition appears
277	// — i.e. the hostname never resolved to a usable address in the window, which is
278	// exactly what the bogus_name subtest expects to observe and report.
279	func firstResolutionAfter(journal string, after time.Time) (endpoint string, elapsed time.Duration, ok bool) {
280		for _, line := range strings.Split(journal, "\n") {
281			if !strings.Contains(line, "first endpoint resolution") {
282				continue
283			}
284			var rec dnsResolutionRecord
285			if err := json.Unmarshal([]byte(line), &rec); err != nil {
286				continue
287			}
288			if rec.ToEndpoint == "" || !rec.Time.After(after) {
289				continue
290			}
291			return rec.ToEndpoint, rec.Time.Sub(after), true
292		}
293		return "", 0, false
294	}
295	
296	// dnsTrafficSample runs a short best-effort iperf3 TCP sample from edge to the
297	// concentrator's inner IP and reports the achieved throughput. Unlike iperfTCP
298	// (smoke tier), every failure is LOGGED and returns ok=false rather than
299	// t.Fatalf — the whole point of this tier is that a data-plane sample taken after a
300	// bogus-name dial REPORTS, it never fails the suite.
301	func dnsTrafficSample(t *testing.T, r *Runner, edge Host, serverIP string) (mbps float64, ok bool) {
302		t.Helper()
303		ctx, cancel := context.WithTimeout(context.Background(), dnsTrafficTimeout)
304		defer cancel()
305		res, err := r.Run(ctx, edge, fmt.Sprintf("iperf3 -c %s -t 5 -J", serverIP))
306		if err != nil {
307			t.Logf("edge: dial-by-name traffic sample failed: %v", err)
308			return 0, false
309		}
310		var rep iperfReport
311		if err := json.Unmarshal([]byte(res.Stdout), &rep); err != nil {
312			t.Logf("edge: parse dial-by-name iperf3 JSON failed: %v\n%s", err, res.Stdout)
313			return 0, false
314		}
315		return rep.End.SumSent.BitsPerSecond / 1e6, true
316	}
